title: Anti-corruption agency
description: A separate agency for prevention and enforcement, intended to ensure autonomy, expertise, and "fresh start" accountability.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Combating Corruption: What the Ecuadorian Anti-Corruption Agency Can Learn from International Good Practice," Maria del Mar Landette, University of Birmingham School of Public Policy, International Development Department, 70 pages, Sept 2002.

http://www.yachana.org/ecuatorianistas/landette.pdf

The core of this dissertation is the analysis of anti-corruption agencies and their effectiveness as tools to combat corruption in the public sector.  The cases of the national anti-corruption agencies of Hong Kong, Singapore, Botswana and Ecuador are examined, and key variables in their structure and organizational arrangements compared to determine best practices that canbe applied by the Ecuadorian anti-corruption agency in its fight against public sector corruption.

 

#2 "Role of an Anti-Corruption Agency in the Struggle Against Corruption: The Case of the Serious Office in Ghana," Sam K. Asibuo, 11 pages, 2001.

http://unpan1.un.org/intradoc/groups/public/documents/CAFRAD/UNPAN002272.pdf

This paper examines the effectiveness of the Serious Fraud Office (SFO) as an institution for fighting corruption in Ghana . The functions, powers, limitations and difficulties of the SFO are also discussed.  The paper concludes with some suggestions for improving the work of the SFO.

 

#3 "Chapter 11: Independent Anti-Corruption Agencies," TI Source Book 2000, Transparency International, 10 pages, 2000.

http://www.transparency.org/sourcebook/11.pdf

Chapter 11 of the TI Source Book presents a framework for establishing an effective national anti-corruption agency.  It discusses specific questions such as checks and balances, retroactivity, protecting informers, monitoring assets of public sector decision-makers, and public procurement.  The final section includes indicators for assessing anti-corruption agencies.

 


***************************************************
CASE STUDY: The ANTI-Corruption Agency of Malayasia
(from UNODC, 2004, UN ANTICORRUPTION TOOLKIT)
The Anti-Corruption Agency (ACA) of Malaysia was founded in 1967 by merging three earlier bodies. The main functions of ACA are to:

•           Investigate and prosecute offences of corruption;

•           Prevent and curb corruption in the public service; and

•           Investigate the conduct of civil servants.

Corruption is defined in the Prevention of Corruption Act 1961 and Ordinance 22, 1971, as including bribery, false claims and the use of public position or office for pecuniary or other advantage. Claims for false expenses are dealt with by ACA, but the police deal with other fraudulent claims.

ACA prosecutes offenders and seeks to prevent corruption. In its early years, it carried out many investigations against members of the public for bribing civil servants. Subsequently, as it instituted preventive programmes to encourage the public to report such corrupt practices, an increasing number of civil servants were arrested. Although much of its present work is concerned with public servants, ACA has also investigated ministers, charged a football player with rigging a ma h result, and had bank managers convicted for taking a personal percentage in exchange for agreeing to grant bank loans. As at August 1994 there were 150 court hearings a month. A promotional video is used to seek cooperation from the public and to deter those who are tempted by corrupt acts. The ACA also provides advice on management methods to reduce opportunities for corruption.

Activities of the Anti Corruption Agency (ACA)
The activities of ACA include:

•           Procuring intelligence and investigating corruption cases;

•           Anti-corruption campaigns, education, television programmes and other publicity;

•           Prosecuting offenders;

•           Studying weaknesses in government administration; and

•           Conducting such activities as surprise corruption checks.

ACA investigates conflicts of interest, extortion, false claims and corrupt business transactions. Prevention and deterrence techniques include punishment, management and education, and enlisting public support to fight corruption. One ACA officer has described corruption as a "consensual crime" with its own natural defence mechanisms, and has complained that conviction sentences were often "too light". Between 1985 and 1990, half of those convicted received a one-day imprisonment sentence only, and 85 per cent received sentences of less than six months.

Increasing responsibilities have been given to the ACA. They include the adoption of revised regulations for conduct and discipline of public officers in 1993, a code of ethics for judges in 1994, and increased cooperation with religious organizations. A new division of the ACA was formed in 1996 to provide an early warning system for corruption in large Government corporations. In April 1997, the Government endorsed a three-pronged strategy for the ACA  to strengthen its resources and management, further develop its preventive and promotional work, and improve enforcement, including redrafting of laws on corruption. In 1999-2000, the ACA took responsibility for attacking corruption in the private sector, and sought extra staff for that purpose.

The ACA has given special attention to the "top ten" corruption prone agencies in Malaysia, to the setting up of Ethics, Quality and Productivity Committees at State and departmental levels; and to the interests and safety of witnesses and informers.  Meanwhile, the civil service has developed a set of values known as "The Twelve Pillars" to which civil servants subscribe:

1                     The value of time

2                     The success of perseverance

3                     The pleasure of working

4                     The dignity of simplicity

5                     The worth of character

6                     The power of kindness        

7                     The influence of example

8                     The obligation of duty

9                     The wisdom of economy

10                 The virtue of patience

11                 The improvement of talent

12                 The joy of originating

 

 Mandate of the Anti-Corruption Agency
The ACA has the power to investigate, interrogate, arrest and prosecute. Staff members were appointed initially by transfers from the police but are now recruited into a separate administration. They receive public-sector pay plus an incentive allowance. There are six divisions: Prosecutions; Investigations; Information; Prevention; Training; and Administration. Legislation, regulation, operation and motivation are closely linked.  Thus, customs officers at the checkpoints and police on the street are allowed to carry only a small amount of cash on their person. Random checks and searches provide evidence of corrupt cash payments and discourage acceptance of bribes.

 

Increased Emphasis on Prevention
The ACA acts on information received: it receives 8,000 reports a year. Only a small number of those reports are found to be mischievous. ACA uses paid informers in ways that are described as being "similar to the FBI".  Initially, information is received in confidence but subsequent enquiries are made openly. The three stages are discreet enquiry, preliminary enquiry and open enquiry. Publicity for enquiries may encourage others to come forward with evidence. The aims of investigation are to prosecute, uncover breaches in civil-service discipline, propose improvements to systems to reduce opportunities for corruption, assist other agencies, for example the Inland Revenue, and  cultivate future information sources. Informants may be anonymous. Publicity for enquiries as well as for charges, trials and convictions, discourages corrupt practice.

Greater emphasis is now given to preventing corruption than in the past, with a three-pronged strategy of Information, Education and Communication (I, E, C). Efforts to educate the public and discourage people from conducting corrupt practices are based primarily on ethics and religion. The thrust of ACA work on prevention was presented in a promotional video made available in 1994. The video includes quotes from the sayings of the Prophet, "Allah curses the giver of bribes and the receiver of bribes and the person who paves the way for both parties". The underlying causes of corruption are described as living beyond one's income and running heavy debts, with corruption breeding off administrative weaknesses. Efforts are made to appeal to people to avoid corruption and are based on morality ("Corruption is evil"), social pressure ("Would you support your family with money derived from corruption?"), self-respect ("We have an image to keep as Government servants") and loyalty. Corruption is said to be dangerous because it is infectious. The video makes an appeal to the public to cooperate in fighting corruption ("Have you reported an act of corruption to the authorities?"). The video warns that corruption does not pay, and presents a scene of a clanging door bell in a prison, which represents a threat of punishment, and the scene of an arrest in front of a family, which attempts to bring a sense of shame. ACA also uses television dramas.

There is concern about the slow progress of cases through the courts and the lack of severity in sentencing. Other problems listed by the ACA include the transaction of corrupt money in foreign countries, the fact that the public does "not want to get involved", difficulty in getting cooperation from foreign citizens and organizations, fear of vengeance for supporting the authority, difficulties in retaining witnesses and people accepting corruption "as a way of problem solving ... and convenience".  Another problem is the allegation that ACA is a tool of the Government, and that it arrests small fish but lets the "whales" get off free. ACA has responded to this allegation by claiming that two State ministers have been prosecuted and a senior cabinet minister investigated and, although the latter was cleared of any criminal offence, he was asked to declare all his assets (ACA Annual Report 1993). It is, however, recognized that the "businessman-politician" is hard to catch.

ACA staff believes that reducing the levels of corruption depends on:

•           The political will;

•           A Malaysian requirement that public servants may not run their own businesses;

•           A requirement that public servants should declare their assets;

•           A check to ensure that public servants do not have a lifestyle that is beyond their means; and

•           A rule that those that are too heavily in debt may not be promoted.

ACA staff is recruited at levels equivalent to police sergeants, inspectors and assistant superintendents. Initial on-the-job training is provided, followed by specialist courses on prosecution, intelligence gathering, prevention and management. Some staff go overseas to obtain academic qualifications in such fields as criminology. Training opportunities are sought in the United Kingdom and the United States. The ACA  manpower and budget come under the  Department of the Prime Minister. The Director General is appointed by the King on the advice of the Prime Minister and reports to Parliament. The ACA cooperates closely with similar organizations in many countries.

The ACA is vigilant about the possibility of its own staff being corrupted and checks are made.. The police retain the power to charge people with corruption, including ACA staff. The public may complain about ACA staff to the Public Complaints Bureau.

title: Ombudsmen
description: Individuals or agencies with general powers who provide a means for impartial and independent investigation of complaints against Government agencies and their employees.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 Fighting Corruption: The Namibian Experience and the Role of the Office of the Ombudsman, Adv. Bience Gawanas, 8th International Anti-Corruption Conference, 1997.

http://www.transparency.org/iacc/8th_iacc/papers/gawanas.html 

This paper enumerates the powers and responsibilities that the Office of the Ombudsman holds in Namibia. Besides responding to alleged wrongdoing, the Office tries to proactively create good governance, an ethical climate, a human rights culture, and appropriate checks and balances.

 

#2 "The Long Way of the Emerging Ombudsman Institution in Bulgaria," Center for the Study of Democracy, 6 pages, May 2004.

http://www.csd.bg/fileSrc.php?id=513

This paper discusses the failure to establish an effective ombudsman institution in Bulgaria, enumerating the reasons for failure and outlining recommendations for an improved process.

 

#3 UN Ombudsman

http://www.un.org/ombudsman

The UN Ombudsman has a broad mandate to look into problems within the UN in a confidential manner to assist in their informal resolution. The Ombudsman is also mandated to make recommendations aimed at improving the organization.

title: Audit institutions
description: Verification of records, processes or functions by an independent entity.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Audit of Fraud - Case Study by SAI India," I.P. Singh, Anti-Corruption Summit 2000.

http://www.respondanet.com/english/anti_corruption/reports/presentation_materials/Singh2-Speech.doc

Nearly seven billion rupees were fraudulently drawn from the treasuries by the officials of a provincial government department.  SAI India conducted an investigation into the fraudulent transactions and brought out a special report. During the investigations, conventional fraud auditing techniques were supported by extensive use of computerized database. This summary describes the fraud, the techniques used in the investigation, and the dramatic results.

 

#2 "Results-Oriented Public Expenditure: Case Study of Mali," Overseas Development Institute 2003

http://www.odi.org.uk/publications/working_papers/wp206_web.pdf

Mali introduced a system of programme budgeting in 1998, which involves three-year rolling expenditure frameworks that are based on long-term strategic plans where these exist. While systems for verifying outputs and outcomes remain inadequate, recent initiatives are improving the availability of survey-based social and welfare indicators.

title: Strengthening judicial institutions
description: Aims to strengthen integrity, educate judges about the nature and extent of corruption, and establish adequate accountability structures.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Case Study on Legal and Judicial Reform: Regional Cooperation," William T. Loris, World Bank Conference: Empowerment, Security And Opportunity Through Law And Justice, July 2001.

http://www4.worldbank.org/legal/ljr_01/doc/Loris.pdf

During the transition to a free market economy in Central Europe, the IDLI-EBRD-Central European Initiative Commercial Law Project was conceived and carried out. This case study outlines the essential elements of that project and attempts to identify certain lessons learned that may help in similar ongoing efforts in the region and elsewhere.

 

#2 "Process drives success: Key lessons from a decade of legal reform," David S. Bernstein, Chief Counsel, EBRD, 2002, 12 pages.

http://www.ebrd.com/country/sector/law/about/lessons.pdf

This article identifies three key lessons for institutions and agencies providing legal reform assistance. It argues that a successful legal reform project is one that adapts internationally accepted principles and standards to the local legal environment, focuses time and resources on implementation and enforcement and, most importantly, works through an open, transparent and inclusive process.

 

#3 "Lessons Learned in Instituting Legal Reforms: Study from Georgia," Gia Kiknadze, 9th International Anti-Corruption Conference, Transparency International, October 1999.

http://www.transparency.org/iacc/9th_iacc/papers/day1/ws2/d1ws2_gkiknadze.html

This paper discusses a number of different anti-corruption measures in Georgia, including legal reform, which provided independence to judges and gave them appropriate legal and social guarantees. It cancelled the state monopoly on arbitrage cases, also established the appellation court institution and strictly identified professional and moral criteria for appointing and electing the judges.  Six hundred and fifty new laws and 10 new codes have been introduced, which entirely replaced the old system and paved the way for Georgia's legal system to become harmonised with European laws.

title: Civil service reform
description: Aims to reduce incentives and discretion of public employees while increasing oversight, accountability and benefits for honest employees.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "The Part Played by Structural Reforms of the State in Fighting Corruption: The Case in Uganda," Hon. A. Ruzindana, 8th International Anti-Corruption Conference, 1997.

http://www.transparency.org/iacc/8th_iacc/papers/ruzindana.html

This paper presents a discussion of the structural reforms that have taken place in Uganda and their impact on curbing corruption.  Several reform elements involving the civil service are included.  Few other countries on the continent have carried out a public sector reform initiative of this scale and with so many components, but the task ahead remains difficult.

 

#2 "Administrative and Civil Service Reform in Equatorial Guinea," by Guillermo Mangué Nnandongo, African Training and Research Centre in Administration for Development, Morocco, 2002.

http://unpan1.un.org/intradoc/groups/public/documents/cafrad/unpan005513.pdf

This case study analyzes an unsuccessful instance of civil service reform, with lessons for other LDCs. In Equatorial Guinea, since the attainment of independence in 1968, the public administration system has been subjected to numerous changes in structure, operating style and orientation. However, these have not been very successful, in part because the public administration in Equatorial Guinea did not make concerted effort to create a functional framework in which government institutions would achieve their long-term objectives. Successful reform would require that civil servants be fully involved, the introduction and effective implementation of appropriate incentives schemes, relevant recruitment procedures, as well as the allocation of appropriate human, financial and material resources for the design and implementation of the programs.

title: Codes and standards of conduct
description: Establish general standards of behaviour consistent with basic ethical principles which may be applied in both the public or private sectors.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Ethics and Corruption: What is an 'Ethics Infrastructure'?" OECD, Feb 2001.

http://www1.oecd.org/puma/ethics/infras.htm

This brief overview describes eight key elements of an ethics infrastructure.

 

#2 "Corporate Codes of Conduct," ILO Bureau for Workers' Activities, Online July 2004.

http://www.itcilo.it/actrav/actrav-english/telearn/global/ilo/code/main.htm

This overview describes the concepts, background, content, and implementation of corporate codes of conduct.

 

************************************************************************************

CASE STUDY: Nigeria: Development of a code of conduct
(from UNODC, 2004, UN ANTICORRUPTION TOOLKIT)
 

In the case study below, Jeremy Pope of Transparency International describes how he, with the direct support of the newly elected President of Nigeria, Olusegun Obasanjo, involved all the key stakeholders in developing, from scratch, a code of conduct for Government ministers.

Background
As he assumed office, the newly elected President of Nigeria, Olusegun Obasanjo, faced a daunting task.  For a generation, his country had been plundered by a series of military administrations. During that time, the civil service had become demoralized and dysfunctional. There were few permanent secretaries who had ever written a cabinet memorandum as the Cabinet had not met as such in their professional careers. Moreover, few even knew how a law was enacted as the military had “governed” by decree. While there were some outstanding individuals of probity and dedication to public service, there had really been no democratic "government" at all in the accepted sense of the word: no unity of purpose, no teamwork, no sense of cohesion and certainly little evidence of any commitment to promoting the public interest. There had simply been a succession of appointees who had seen high political office as nothing more and nothing less, than a highway to self-enrichment and the bestowing of favour on friends and relations. It was in such an unpromising environment that the newly elected President vowed to return his country to democratic rule.

Because of the tradition of self-enrichment, it was widely believed that many of those who had sought election to the national assembly and aspired to serve as ministers were captives of the old ethos; while they might be willing to articulate the new ethos they might not have much belief in it. Furthermore, it was understood that many had borrowed heavily to finance their various campaigns and were effectively in hock to vested interests who were expecting a handsome return on their "investments". Others were the beneficiaries of corrupt practices under previous regimes and were seeking political power in order to protect what they had acquired illicitly.

Challenges
It was essential to overcome distorted political values, and make the new Cabinet internalize the "Obasanjo ethic". The challenge was to start a process that, if successful, would completely revolutionize the understanding, deeply entrenched in the political life of the nation, that ministerial office was a "licence" to dispense favours to family and friends without regard to the public interest or the ability of the nation to bear the costs involved. Clearly, whatever else might be involved, the start had to be made at the top.

 

Process
For that reason, as soon as the senate was selected and confirmed, and before portfolios had been allocated and the new ministers were formally sworn in, the newly elected President convened a "values retreat" for his incoming team. The objective was to create an atmosphere in which the new ministers could reflect on the tasks that lay before them and how they might achieve sustainable and meaningful change to the culture of corruption that gripped most, if not all, of their departments and agencies. Just what this would entail was unknown.  The retreat would last for two days, and the President was venturing into uncharted waters.

As the outcomes desired included a code of conduct for ministers and senior advisers, it was decided to draft a code of conduct for the retreat before it was held.  As it was vital for the document to be a product of the ministers themselves, and not simply imposed by the President, the "draft" was transformed into a "questionnaire" that asked ministers and advisers how strongly they felt about each proposition contained in the draft. The  questionnaire was distributed as the retreat began and it was completed straight away.

As expected, the ministers, in their answers to the questions, almost universally endorsed as the propositions contained in the "draft code", and as a result, when the "draft code", amended somewhat in the light of the responses, was placed before them, it was already a familiar document and chimed well with the opinions they had already expressed.

Before the "draft" was reprocessed in this way, however, the ministerial team and their advisers had to address the "values" issue, and it was uncertain how this could be done. In the event, the options of a rousing "anti-corruption" address from the President and/or an address by someone else, whether Nigerian or an external resource person, were both rejected as simply stating the obvious: that ministers should not act corruptly but  discharge public duties in a manner consistent with public trust.

Instead, the strategy was to divide the team into small groups and invite them to answer a series of short but challenging questions in the expectation that they would in effect lecture themselves even more effectively than any platform speaker could. The questions were:

•           What particular action by a particular minister in the past did they strongly  approve of?

•           Why did they strongly approve of it?

•           What particular action by a particular minister in the past did they strongly  disapprove of?

•           Why did they strongly disapprove of it?

•           How in a sentence would each of them like to be remembered for their period in office as a minister/senior adviser?

Each group had a highly animated discussion, and it emerged from the conclusions that the most admired ministers were those who were:

•           Modest, honest and saw themselves as servants of the people of Nigeria;

•           Committed to their portfolios, minimized waste and made a positive difference to the lives of the people that their ministries served;

•           Punctual in their own timekeeping and did not waste the  time of others by keeping them waiting;

•           Respectful of the law and, in particular, did not flout their official positions by, for example, ignoring traffic lights; and

•           Respectful of other people in public places and did not, for example, jump the queue at the airport.

Results
Against the background of such conclusions, the groups reassembled to consider the draft code of conduct. It was examined in a plenary session, and the parameters of each paragraph were explored, discussed and revised until universal agreement was reached on the text.

At that point, President Obasanjo, who had deliberately remained absent from the discussions so as not to inhibit the freedom of discussion, rejoined the meeting.  He welcomed the adoption of the code and suggested that all of them, including him, should personally sign it. That was done, and they then signed a second copy, that was handed to the President to retain.

Finally, the President indicated his satisfaction with the outcome of the retreat and elicited from the collective meeting the response that those who failed to uphold the spirit and the letter of the code would have let the whole ministerial team down and would thus be required to resign. To facilitate that process, should it prove necessary, the ministers would, prior to being sworn in,  sign an undated letter of resignation that the President would hold in safe custody.

The full text of the new code was then released to the media, and it was carried prominently throughout the country, with the more serious newspapers reproducing the text in its entirety. 

That was the beginning of the process. The code had been discussed, internalized and adopted by the ministers as their own document. Their individual commitment to its provisions had been personally expressed in writing. The code had been widely published and, by implication, the media and the wider general public had been invited to measure the performance of each and every minister against its provisions.

To enable ministers and senior advisers to seek guidance on any particular dilemmas that they, individually, might face from time to time, the code included provision for the appointment by the President of an independent source of advice, namely a person of high public standing and reputation. Those who sought his/her advice were open in disclosing all relevant facts and, by acting on the advice they received, would have a complete answer to any subsequent allegation of misconduct. 

That was the beginning of what was planned to be a sustained and systematic assault on a culture of political and administrative corruption. Starting at the top, the plan was to drive the values down through the management systems, with the leaders secure in the possession of the necessary moral authority that would enable them both to provide leadership and to take disciplinary decisions where these were called for.

The exercise did not end there. Plans have now been advanced for workshops along similar lines, including case studies, to be conducted throughout the highest echelons of the public service.  Thereafter, the ethical approach will be institutionalized downwards and throughout the public service.

On a general note, the biggest risk of any code of conduct is that:

•           It is not accepted or even known by the stakeholders who are supposed to be governed by it; or

•           It might be known to the stakeholders but is not monitored in an adequate manner.

•           There are no risks, costs and/or uncertainties associated with breaking the code of conduct.

The approach described in the Nigeria case study tries to address the concerns by:

•           Involving the key players in the development of the code of conduct;

•           Disseminating the code of conduct to the public to create awareness about what they can expect, and thereby provide some checks and balances; and involving a strong President in the process to make the code of conduct more serious, almost as if it were a social contract.

title: National anti-corruption commissions
description: Intended to develop anti-corruption strategy and its major elements, set basic priorities, sequence strategic elements, monitor progress in specific areas and adjust planning and timelines to progress.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 Hong Kong Independent Commission Against Corruption

http://www.icac.org.hk/eng/main/index.html

The ICAC was set up in Hong Kong in 1974 to combat widespread corruption.  The web site contains detailed information about the Commission's history and activities, including annual reports and policy objectives. 

 

#2 "BAC Report Part II: Proposed Structure of Independent Anti-Corruption Commission (IACC)," Khan Sarwar Murshid, Transparency International Bangladesh, 2002.

http://unpan1.un.org/intradoc/groups/public/documents/APCITY/UNPAN004889.pdf

Recognizing the drawbacks of Bangladesh's Bureau of Anti-Corruption because it was not an independent agency, Transparency International Bangladesh proposed the establishment of an Independent Anti-Corruption Commission.  This proposal presents the background and need for a commission, and a proposed structure.

title: Empowering local governments
description: A suite of tools specifically developed or tailored to support action on a variety of fronts at the local level.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Bottom up administrative reform: Using Local Governance Scorecards in the Nigeria Community and Local Government Development Project," Talib Esmail , Nick Manning and Jana Orac, 16 pages, July 2001.

http://www1.worldbank.org/publicsector/civilservice/LGAindicators_0718.pdf

This report describes the development of indicators and a methodology for using governance scorecards to strengthen local governments in Nigeria.

 

#2 "The Bhaktapur Framework,"Julia Steets, TI Nepal, 2 Pages, August 2001.

http://www.transparency.org/building_coalitions/public/local_goverment/documents/Nepal_Case.rtf

TI Nepal has developed the Bhaktapur Framework to introduce anti-corruption measures at the local level in Nepal.  This summary describes the concept, implementation steps, and lessons and recommendations related to the framework.

title: Strengthening legislatures
description: Designed to increase the integrity of parliament by increasing accountability, public awareness and public access.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "USAID's Experience Strengthening Legislatures," Center for Democracy and Governance, 36 pages, June 2001.

http://www.usaid.gov/our_work/democracy_and_governance/publications/pdfs/pnach308.pdf

This document provides an overview of USAID's experience in legislative strengthening.  It describes what the Agency has done and is now doing to promote responsive and effective legislatures.  It highlights ways in which improved legislative performance has strengthened democracy.  It does not provide any evaluation of the relative merits or cost benefit analysis of the various approaches.  Rather, it is a summary of approaches that have been tried by USAID.

 

#2 "Strengthening legislatures: implications from industrial countries," World Bank PREM Note #63, 4 pages, March 2002.

http://www1.worldbank.org/prem/PREMNotes/premnote63.pdf

This PREM note, produced for the World Bank, argues that legislative performance in industrial countries offers a framework for examining legislative reform in developing and transition economies.  The note examines the degree of legislative independence, through looking at examples in the United States, Germany and the United Kingdom, and then looks at the rights of opposition parties.  It concludes with lessons learned from the experiences of several industrialized countries, and suggestions for further reading.

 

#3 "Parliaments: Tools and Tasks for Controlling Corruption," Robert Miller and Rick Stapenhurst, 9 pages.

http://www.worldbank.org/wbi/governance/pdf/parl_booklet.pdf

This paper describes ways that Parliament can work actively against corruption.  It discusses how to build political will, the importance of transparency, specific parliamentary anti-corruption tools, and a list of potential action steps.

 

#4 "USAID Handbook on Legislative Strengthening," Center for Democracy and Governance, 82 pages, Feb 2000.

http://www.usaid.gov/our_work/democracy_and_governance/publications/pdfs/pnacf632.pdf

This handbook is designed to assist USAID Missions in developing strategies to help legislatures function more effectively and to perform their functions more democratically and representatively. It explains the importance of legislatures in a democracy, describes structural and other factors that influence legislative behavior, and enumerates problems legislatures commonly face. It then presents an assessment framework that is designed to help USAID field officers pinpoint the weaknesses and understand the capabilities of their host-country legislature. The following section identifies various assistance activities designed to address the weaknesses and take advantage of strengths discovered in the assessment. The handbook concludes with a number of considerations for implementing legislative programs and a presentation of emerging issues in legislative strengthening.

 

#5 "e-Parliaments: The Use of ICTs to Improve Parliamentary Processes,"

Tess Kingham, World Bank Institute, 2001.

http://siteresources.worldbank.org/WBI/Resources/wbi37210Kingham.pdf

This paper examines the progress of the debate on the impact of Information and Communication Technology [ICT] on governance and the possible future influence of ICT on the development of democratic systems of Government.

title: Public financial disclosures
description: Aims to increase transparency with respect to the incomes and assets of public servants.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Instruction Manual: Public Official Financial Disclosure," Alaska Public Offices Commission, 39 pages, Jan 2004.

 http://www.state.ak.us/apoc/pdf/coiall04.pdf

The instruction manual for public official financial disclosure in Alaska describes reasons for financial disclosure, who is required to disclose, and detailed instructions about completing the form.

 

#2 "Public Officer Ethics Act Provisions for Declarations of Income, Assets, and Liabilities Evaluation and Recommendations," James Luh, Transparency International, 8 pages, July 2003.

http://www.tikenya.org/documents/assetdeclaration.pdf

This paper evaluates the effectiveness of Kenya's Public Officer Ethics Act, which provided for wealth declaration requirements.  While the Act is a good starting point, serious shortcomings include not providing for public access to officials’ asset declarations, not spelling out clearly what assets, liabilities, and interests public, and failing to provide a framework for review or inspection of asset disclosing their wealth to the public.

title: Monitoring authority for public sector contracts
description: National or international authority with mechanisms to guarantee the honesty and transparency of public-sector contracts and arbitration.

title: Procurement process reform
description: Aims to instill a transparent and efficient procurement process with systematic and dependable procedures, including records explaining and justifying all actions.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Towards More Transparent Budgeting and Public Procurement in Municipalities in Serbia," Julia Steets, TI Serbia, 3 Pages, August 2001.

http://www.transparency.org/building_coalitions/public/local_goverment/documents/Serbia_Case.rtf

In order to increase the efficiency and quality of municipal services, to improve the communications and relations between the municipal administration and citizens and to establish a more efficient and transparent budgeting and public procurement system that would help to curb corruption and attract investment, TI Serbia developed the program Towards More Transparent Budgeting and Public Procurement in Municipalities in Serbia.This summary presents the concept, implementation steps, and lessons and recommendations related to the program.

 

#2 "Programme for Transparent Contracting," Julia Steets, TI Argentina, 3 Pages, August 2001.

http://www.transparency.org/building_coalitions/public/local_goverment/documents/Argentina_Case.rtf

As part of a more comprehensive Integrity System for Local Governments, TI Argentina (Poder Ciudadano) has developed a mechanism for rendering public procurement more transparent, proposing a program with two main components: Public Hearings and Integrity Pacts.This summary presents the concept, implementation steps, and lessons and recommendations related to the program.

title: Simplification of bureaucratic structures and procedures
description: Endeavours to reduce discretion of public officials by identifying administrative steps towards permit issuance and, if useful, simplifying administrative flow charts.

title: Reducing and structuring discretion
description: Entails improved access to information, identification of bottlenecks, analysis of behavioural patterns, and quality control techniques.

title: Results- or fact-based management
description: Management structures that set clear goals for achievement, as well as criteria and processes for assessing whether they have, in fact, been achieved.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Promoting Results Based Management in the Public Sectors of Developing Countries," Cedric Saldanha, presented at the World Bank Roundtable on Better Measuring, Monitoring, and Managing for Results, 22 pages, June 2002.

http://www.adb.org/Governance/results_based_mgt.pdf

This paper presents challenges to, key elements of, and specific indicators for establishing results based management in public sectors of developing countries. 

 

#2 "Results-Based Management and Accountability for Enhanced Aid Effectiveness," Réal Lavergne, CIDA Policy Branch, 45 pages, July 2002.

http://www.sti.ch/pdfs/swap052e.pdf

This document begins with a general discussion of RBM and Accountability issues in CIDA.  This is

followed by an exploration of recent trends in thinking about aid effectiveness, both internationally and in

the Agency.  The implications of these trends for RBM and accountability are then reviewed in three

major sections focused on issues of local ownership, control and accountability, the implications of CD

for the logic of RBM and performance measurement, reporting, evaluation and audit.  The paper ends

with some general conclusions and a synthesis of recommendations.

title: Incentives to improve employee culture and motivation
description: Provide incentives such as increased remuneration, remuneration more closely linked to positive performance, increased or enhanced professional status and improved job security and working conditions.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Anti-Corruption Incentives and Constituencies in the Post-Communist Region," Rasma Karklins Collegium Budapest, draft, 16 pages, Sept 2002.

http://www.colbud.hu/honesty-trust/karklins/pub03.pdf

This paper recognizes that the involvement of civil society can serve as an incentive for non-corrupt politics and civil cooperation.  The analysis focuses on identifying significant anti-corruption constituencies in order to change the political culture.

 

#2 "Political and Economic Incentives during an Anti-Corruption Crackdown," Rafael Di Tella and Ernesto Schargrodsky, 23 pages, Jan 2002.

http://www.utdt.edu/~eschargr/Political%20and%20Economic%20Incentives.PDF

This study analyzes an anti-corruption crackdown in Buenos Aires, in which hospitals were given political and economic incentives to reduce corruption.   Each hospital

had to report the purchase prices paid for a sample of inputs acquired through a

decentralized procurement process.  Using this information, the Health Secretariat

compiled price comparisons highlighting the hospitals paying the lowest and highest

values for each product. This paper analyzes the economic incentives of the procurement officers under

monitoring, and the political incentives of the political officers that implemented these

controls.  The evidence shows that the policy induced an immediate fall in input

prices.  After this initial fall, prices rose, but they were still lower than those

prevailing before the crackdown.  The empirical results also show that the hospitals

with the lower procurement officers’ wages were the first hospitals where prices

started to rise.  An explanation is provided for these findings drawing on two previous

papers by the authors.

title: Access to information
description: Aims to inform the public about governance issues that affect them directly, increasing expectations and pressure on officials to meet high standards.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Open Information Programme," Julia Steets, TI Latvia, 3 Pages, August 2001.

http://www.transparency.org/building_coalitions/public/local_goverment/documents/Latvia_Case.rtf

TI Latvia has identified transparency and access to information as its starting point to create accountability and curb corruption. This summary presents the concept, implementation steps, and lessons and recommendations related to the program.

title: Public awareness campaign
description: Production of relevant governance information for public consumption that highlights the true costs, nature and variations, stigma and punishments and ongoing efforts to fight corruption.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Increasing Transparency of the Democratically Elected Institutions by Educating the Members of These Institutions," Julia Steets, TI Estonia, 2 Pages, August 2001.

http://www.transparency.org/building_coalitions/public/local_goverment/documents/Estonia_Case.rtf

As a starting point for its anti-corruption initiatives, TI Estonia focused on raising the awareness of local officials and citizens about the harmful effects of corruption. Tactics included a media campaign, anti-corruption workshops for local government officials, and a public opinion survey to identify the main problems of local governments. This summary presents the concept, implementation steps, and lessons and recommendations related to the program.

 

#2 "Communication Strategies for Municipal Governments in the Czech Republic," Julia Steets, TI Czech Republic, 3 Pages, August 2001.

http://www.transparency.org/building_coalitions/public/local_goverment/documents/Czech_Republic_Case.rtf

TI Czech Republic (TIC) launched an initiative aimed at improving access to information in municipalities in the Czech Republic. The initiative focused on the implementation of public access to information legislation in local governments.  This summary presents the concept, implementation steps, and lessons and recommendations related to the program.

title: Media training and investigative journalism
description: Ensure that journalists have the skills necessary to investigate, appraise and report to the public on corruption issues in an independent and useful manner.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1"Curbing Corruption: The Media's Work, Recommendations for Action," Frank Vogl, TI Working Paper, 1999.

http://www.transparency.org/working_papers/vogl-recommendations/index.html#download
Frank Vogl calls for a detailed discussion on media ownership, budgets, and bribery within the media to establish its role in combating corruption.  The emphasis of the paper is on "non-media developments” such as embracing press freedom laws and developing international initiatives to strengthen the independence of the media, raising awareness of the media's importance, and privatizing the media under strong anti-trust enforcements.  Vogl’s main assumption is that the autonomy of the media lies in a series of interrelated factors that can only be solved as a whole. Accordingly, Vogl recommends setting a common agenda between all interested parties and building coalitions among judges, prosecutors, regulators and journalists.

 

#2 "The media’s role: covering or covering up corruption?" Bettina Peters, in Global Corruption Report, 13 pages, 2003. http://www.globalcorruptionreport.org/download/gcr2003/06_Medias_role_(Peters).pdf

This article gives an overview of the problems journalists encounter when reporting corruption as well as the means to secure editorial independence in spite of censorship, conflicts of interest and the need to produce material to suit sponsors and advertisers.  The article includes references and links to journalist initiatives to curb corruption in Africa and South America.  Informative boxes describe the state of journalism in Eastern Europe and Brazil, and the results of a survey by the International Public Relations Association, which concluded that no geographical region is free of unethical journalistic practices.

 

#3 Journalist Training to Curb Corruption," David Pezzullo, Economic Perspectives, Vol. 3, No. 5, 4 pages, 1998.

http://usinfo.state.gov/journals/ites/1198/ijee/ijee1198.pdf
This article makes a case for training journalists in investigative journalism and promoting the media as corruption watchdogs.  The training plans for journalists involve teaching about gathering information from the Internet and the need to double-check sources; journalists are also cautioned not to publish unfounded rumors or press releases without their political context.  Journalists are encouraged to raise their sense of professionalism above political loyalties and share information with counterparts from competing media.  Drawing upon his own personal experience in Nicaragua and a comparison with Uganda, Pezzullo argues that while in both countries corruption remains high, the debate on corruption has changed; it is more concrete and the press is more aggressive in denouncing it.

title: Joint government / civil society bodies
description: Boards to monitor the effectiveness of anti-corruption programs, composed of civil society representatives and NGOs specialized in specific areas of public-service delivery.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1. "Civil Society and Public Accountability: The need for active monitoring," Colm Allan, 9th International Anti-Corruption Conference, 1998.

http://www.transparency.org/iacc/9th_iacc/papers/day4/ws8/d4ws8_callan.html

The paper introduces the Public Service Accountability Monitor of the Eastern Cap province of South Africa, which is an initiative intended to give civil society information on actions of civil servants in the province so that they can be held accountable.  Accountability is a two way relationship between public servants and the public at large.  Whilst there is a constitutional obligation on public officials to provide an accountable public service, the onus is on the public to ensure that officials live up to this expectation.  Standards of efficiency and public service delivery can only be as high as the expectations voiced by civil society interest groups.

 

#2 "Local government strengthening, Partnership building," United Nations Global Forum on Innovative Policies and Practices in Local Governance, 1996.

http://magnet.undp.org/Docs/dec/monograph/LocalGovStrength-URT.htm

In Tanzania, the UNDP established the Local Initiative Facility for Urban Environment (LIFE) program as a means to strengthen local governments by assisting local officials in interacting more effectively with the civil society.  The goal of the program is to link civil society with local institutions of governance in such a fashion as to make them both mutually reinforcing and individually and collectively stronger than they might be if they continued to operate independent of each other.  This summary presents a description of the program and its successes.

title: Public complaints mechanisms
description: Enables citizens who become aware of acts of corruption, corrupt practices or general misadministration, to report this to the competent authorities.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Public Complaint Mechanism: India," N. Vittal, Paper presented in the Global Forum on Fighting Corruption And Safeguarding Integrity II, The Hague, 8 pages, May 2001.

http://cvc.nic.in/vscvc/cvcspeeches/sp8may01.pdf
This paper describes the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC), which ensures effective implementation of the Prevention of Corruption Act. The Commission has a range of responsibilities, including the administration of public complaints. Four areas of public complaints activity are explained in the paper: a) the creation of public awareness on the complaints mechanism; b) the protection of those using the mechanism; c) creating confidence in the efficiency of the complaint mechanism; and d) providing access to the most disadvantaged.

 

#2 Considering Complaint Mechanisms: An Important Tool for Code Monitoring and Verification," Nina Ascoly and Ineke Zeldenrust, SOMO, 31 pages, 2003

http://www.somo.nl/monitoring/reports/03-12-complaints_paper.pdf

This paper raises key issues in relation to complaint procedures being developed in conjunction with code compliance monitoring and verification initiatives for the garment and footwear industry.  Examples drawn from the experiences of existing complaint mechanisms are used to highlight these issues.  While this paper does draw upon a variety of experiences it is not intended to be a critique of any existing initiatives, but instead as an input intended to help strengthen these complaint mechanisms

title: Citizens’ charters
description: Establish standards from outside the government, codifying standards and expectations held by citizens in order to empower those citizens who confront corrupt organizations.

title: Financial investigations and the monitoring of assets
description: Includes evaluation of key lifestyle indicators and financial disclosure requirements.

title: Integrity testing
description: Standardized testing procedures to determine whether or not certain public officials engage in corrupt practices.

title: International and regional legal instruments
description: Enlists regional and international partners in the fight against domestic corruption by closing loopholes, overseas shelters, creating extradition agreements, sharing information and resources.

title: National legal instruments
description: Primarily involves criminalization of bribery, embezzlement, and money-laundering in statutes and administrative law to empower law-enforcing entities.

title: Specific controls on laundering
description: Establish a paper trail for all businesses, including all legitimate businesses, thereby creating "structures of global control" in the financial sector.

title: Whistleblower protection
description: Legal protections that encourage people to report crime, civil offences, miscarriages of justice and health and environmental threats by safeguarding them against victimization, dismissal and other forms of reprisal.
content:
Resources and Case Studies	 
#1 "Protection for Whistleblowers", Estelle Feldman, 9th International Anti-Corruption Conference, 1998.

http://www.transparency.org/iacc/9th_iacc/papers/day3/ws7/d3ws7_efeldman.html

After defining the term "whistleblower", Feldman discusses the three stages of whistleblowing: causation, disclosure and retaliation. She also describes different motivations for whistleblowing. The legal provisions for the protection of whistleblowers in the USA, UK and South Africa are reviewed. Feldman emphasizes the importance of the impact of retaliation and victimization on the whisteblower.

 

#2 Whistle Blowing and Corruption: An Initial and Comparative Review," Kirstine Drew, UNICORN Project, 73 pages, Jan 2003.

http://www.psiru.org/reports/2002-08-C-whistle.doc

This report includes general information on whistle blowing, a comparative overview on legislation on the issue featuring different types of information disclosure, a case study on the legislation in the UK, international mechanisms like EU and OECD provisions and organizational initiatives in the World Bank and the European Union.

 

#3 "Corruption and Whistle-Blowing - a Background Note for TUAC," David Hall and Steve Davies, 23 pages, Dec 1999.

http://www1.worldbank.org/publicsector/legal/WhistleblowerTUAC.doc

After giving a summary definition on corruption, its economic risks and penalties as well as international mechanisms to tackle this problem in the private sector like the OECD guidelines, the authors discuss the impact on whistle-blowing on curbing corruption. The typology of whistleblowers they present is broader than in most of the literature covering this phenomenon and justifies the need for different types of legislation on whistle blowing. The authors insist on the necessity of international mechanisms to protect whistle blowers and provide examples of these cases. In the annexes of their report they include a model whistle blowing policy and implementation mechanisms.
